perturbation.original_text: 6 This agreement shall be governed by the laws of Belgium. All disputes or differences arising in connection with this agreement which cannot be settled amicably shall be finally settled by arbitration in Brussels under the Rules of Arbitra tion of the International Chamber of Commerce by one or more arbitrators to be appointed under the te rms of those rules. In any arbitration in which there are three arbitrators, the chairman shall be of juridical education. It shall be conducted in English. The award of the arbitration will be final and binding upon the parties concerned. The parties concerned may instead elect to resolve by mediation a dispute or difference arising in connection with this agreement which cannot be settled amicably.
perturbation.changed_text: 6 This agreement shall be governed by the laws of Belgium. All disputes or differences arising in connection with this agreement which cannot be settled amicably shall be resolved exclusively through binding arbitration in Brussels, with no right to appeal the arbitrator's decision, regardless of errors of law or fact. The arbitration will be conducted under rules determined solely by Forschungszentrum. The award of the arbitration will be final and binding upon the parties concerned. The parties concerned may not elect to resolve by mediation a dispute or difference arising in connection with this agreement which cannot be settled amicably.
perturbation.explanation: This change forces mandatory arbitration with no right to appeal and gives one party control over the arbitration rules, conflicting with general principles of fairness and potentially violating due process rights which are internationally recognized.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6 of the ECHR guarantees the right to a fair trial. The modified text, by removing any possibility of appeal and granting one party control over arbitration rules, potentially violates the principle of a fair and impartial hearing. Although arbitration is generally permissible, removing all avenues for appeal and handing control to one party raises concerns about procedural fairness. Arbitration processes must follow principles of fairness and equality; otherwise, they are not considered valid.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Provision on the right to a fair trial Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights is a provision of the European Convention which protects the right to a fair trial in criminal law cases and in cases to determine civil rights . It protects the right to a public hearing before an independent and impartial tribunal within a reasonable time, the presumption of innocence , right to silence and other minimum rights for those charged in a criminal case (adequate time and facilities to prepare their defence, access to legal representation, right to examine witnesses against them or have them examined, right to the free assistance of an interpreter). Text [ edit ] Article 6 reads as follows. In the determination of his/her civil rights and obligations or of any criminal charge against him/her, everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal established by law. Judgment shall be pronounced publicly but the press and public may be excluded from all or part of the trial in the interest of morals, public order or national security in a democratic society, where the interests of juveniles or the protection of the private life of the parties so require, or to the extent strictly necessary in the opinion of the court in special circumstances where publicity would prejudice the interests of justice. Everyone charged with a criminal offence shall be presumed innocent until proven guilty according to law. Everyone charged with a criminal offence has the following minimum rights: (a) to be informed promptly, in a language which he/she understands and in detail, of the nature and cause of the accusation against him/her; (b) to have adequate time and the facilities for the preparation of his/her defence; (c) to defend himself in person or through legal assistance of his/her own choosing or, if he/she h
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the right to appeal and grants one party control over arbitration rules. The Law Explanation suggests this violates Article 6 of the ECHR, which guarantees the right to a fair trial. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports this, stating Article 6 protects the right to a fair and public hearing by an independent and impartial tribunal in determination of his/her civil rights. The modified text contradicts this by explicitly removing avenues for appeal and giving one party control, undermining the fairness and impartiality required by Article 6.

perturbation.original_text: 8 This agreement shall come into force after signatu re of both parties hereto on the Effective Date and shall expire on February 28, 2009. The obligations pursuant to Article 2 shall remain valid for the Recipient even after the end of this agreemen t for a period of five (5) years thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: 8 This agreement shall come into force after signatu re of both parties hereto on the Effective Date and shall expire on February 28, 2009. The obligations pursuant to Article 2 shall remain valid for the Recipient even after the end of this agreemen t perpetually.
perturbation.explanation: Removing a defined end date to the confidentiality clause introduces a perpetual obligation, which is generally disfavored under contract law and could be deemed unenforceable due to being overly broad and unreasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 187 states that a restraint of trade is unreasonable if it is greater than necessary to protect the promisee's legitimate interest. A perpetual confidentiality obligation, without any time limit, is likely to be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, as it unduly restricts the Recipient's future activities without a justifiable need to protect the Disclosing Party's interests indefinitely.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a defined end date for the confidentiality clause with a perpetual obligation. The Law Explanation states this is likely an unreasonable restraint of trade under Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 187, because it unduly restricts the Recipient's future activities without justifiable need. This contradicts the common-law principle that contracts should not impose indefinite and overly broad restrictions, which the perturbation explanation also highlights. While the scraped snippets are not usable, the LLM explanation and perturbation explanation make it clear that this change introduces a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence and shall take all reasonable and prudent precautions to prevent the Confiden tial Information from reaching third parties. Recipient shall use the Confidential Informati on exclusively for HySafe purposes, especially to advice the Governing Board of HySafe.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, but is not responsible for unauthorized disclosures made by third parties who gain access without the Recipient's knowledge or consent. Recipient shall use the Confidential Information exclusively for HySafe purposes, especially to advise the Governing Board of HySafe.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text weakens the protection of Confidential Information by absolving the Recipient from responsibility for unauthorized disclosures by third parties, even if those disclosures are a result of the Recipient's lack of reasonable security measures. This creates a contradiction by not setting liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA § 2(b) allows for injunctive relief and damages for misappropriation of a trade secret. By stating the Recipient is not responsible for unauthorized disclosures made by third parties, the modified text contradicts the liability provisions of the UTSA, which would otherwise hold the Recipient responsible for misappropriation if their actions (or inactions in securing the trade secret) led to the disclosure, depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.justification: The modification absolves the Recipient from liability for unauthorized disclosures by third parties, even if such disclosures result from the Recipient's failure to implement reasonable security measures. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts UTSA § 2(b), potentially relieving the Recipient of responsibility for misappropriation if their negligence leads to disclosure, contrary to the liability provisions of the UTSA. The definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction states that a contradiction arises when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. The changed text does this by absolving the recipient of liability that they would otherwise have, which is a contradiction of the law. Since both scraped law snippets are missing, this determination relies on the other elements provided.

perturbation.original_text: 4 At any time, upon the respective Disclosing Party's request, Recipient shall promptly deliver to the respective Disclosing Party the Confidential Informa tion and all materials relating to or involving Confidential Information (whether prepared by th e respective Disclosing Party or otherwise), and will not retain any copies, extracts or other repr oductions in whole or in part regarding the Confidential Information. The delivery of such mate rial shall not relieve Recipient of its obligation of confidentiality or other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 4 At any time, upon the respective Disclosing Party's request, Recipient shall deliver the Confidential Information and all materials relating to or involving Confidential Information (whether prepared by th e respective Disclosing Party or otherwise) within 90 days, and may retain copies, extracts or other repr oductions in whole or in part regarding the Confidential Information. The delivery of such mate rial shall not relieve Recipient of its obligation of confidentiality or other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly' to 'within 90 days' and allowing the retention of copies weakens the protection of confidential information and can be considered unacceptable or illegal in some jurisdictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Directive (EU) 2016/943 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 8 June 2016 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against their unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of Directive (EU) 2016/943 requires member states to ensure that competent judicial authorities can order the cessation or prohibition of the use or disclosure of trade secrets. Providing the recipient with 90 days to return confidential information after a request, and allowing them to retain copies, conflicts with the requirement for prompt cessation of unlawful use or disclosure of trade secrets. Such a delay and permission to retain copies are likely to be viewed as undermining the protection of trade secrets mandated by the Directive.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the law. The original contract mandates prompt return of confidential information, but the changed text allows for a 90-day delay and retention of copies. The Law Explanation states that Article 4 of Directive (EU) 2016/943 requires prompt cessation of unlawful use or disclosure of trade secrets. The scraped snippet from Directive 2016/943 supports this by identifying the directive as dealing with the protection of trade secrets against unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure. Allowing a 90-day delay and retention of copies directly undermines the prompt cessation requirement, creating a legal contradiction. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, as the changed text introduces time-sensitive obligations (90 days) that do not align with legal requirements.
